question_text: Which source backbone maps to U02 - Vital Signs II: Reporting and Abnormal Findings?
rationale: U02 is built from Module 10 - Vital Signs and focuses on pulse, respirations, abnormal breathing, combined TPR, blood pressure, pain observations, charting, and reporting.

question_text: Resident B returns from the bathroom with pulse 112, respirations 26 and labored, bluish lips, and states, 'I cannot catch my breath.' What is the best CNA action?
answer_a: Finish routine charting first because the resident just walked.
answer_b: Stay with the resident as safe, notify the licensed nurse or emergency chain immediately, and report the measured facts and resident statement.
answer_c: Tell the resident it is probably anxiety and wait for the breathing to slow.
answer_d: Enter the resident's real name and room number into the Moodle reflection.
rationale: Labored respirations, cyanosis, tachycardia, and a shortness-of-breath statement require prompt reporting and safety support within CNA scope; real PHI is never entered into Moodle.

question_text: While checking an assigned pulse, a CNA notices the rhythm feels newly irregular and the resident says, 'I feel dizzy.' Which response is outside CNA scope?
answer_a: Stay with the resident as safe and report the pulse finding and dizziness promptly.
answer_b: Document objective facts according to facility policy after notifying the nurse.
answer_c: Diagnose the rhythm problem, decide a medication is needed, and delay reporting until the end of shift.
answer_d: Follow care-plan safety measures while waiting for licensed direction.
rationale: CNAs report measured pulse findings, symptoms, and safety concerns; they do not diagnose arrhythmias, make medication decisions, or delay reporting of dizziness with a new irregular pulse.

question_text: Which charting example is most objective for the Resident B scenario?
answer_a: Resident was dramatic and breathing weird after the bathroom.
answer_b: Resident probably had a heart attack and needed oxygen.
answer_c: Family stress caused the resident's breathing problem.
answer_d: 0910: Resident returned from bathroom, pulse 112, respirations 26 and labored, stated 'I cannot catch my breath,' lips appeared slightly bluish; nurse notified.
rationale: Objective charting includes time, activity context, measured values, resident words, observed color and effort, and notification without diagnosis or blame.

question_text: In this unit, what does dyspnea mean?
answer_a: A normal blood-pressure reading after rest
rationale: Dyspnea is difficult or labored breathing, a reportable observation when seen or reported by a resident.

question_text: A resident's blood pressure is much higher than the usual pattern after rest, and the resident reports a severe headache and blurred vision. What should the CNA do?
answer_a: Ignore the reading because blood pressure changes throughout the day.
answer_b: Tell the resident they are having a stroke.
answer_c: Report the reading, timing, position/context, symptoms, and resident statement promptly to the licensed nurse according to policy.
answer_d: Post the numbers in an online course discussion to ask classmates what they mean.
rationale: Unusual blood pressure paired with headache and blurred vision is reportable. The CNA provides context and facts to licensed staff without diagnosing or sharing PHI online.

question_text: Why do CNAs often count respirations quietly after taking a pulse, according to facility policy?
answer_a: Because respirations never need to be reported.
answer_b: Because counting respirations is only for licensed nurses.
answer_c: Because calling attention to breathing can change the resident's breathing pattern and make the count less accurate.
answer_d: Because the CNA should estimate respirations instead of observing them.
rationale: Respirations are often observed discreetly because awareness can alter breathing. The CNA still reports abnormal rate, rhythm, depth, effort, sounds, color, or resident statements.

question_text: A resident reports chest tightness as 7/10, pulse is 104, respirations are 24, and skin appears pale. Which report is best?
answer_a: Resident is probably just seeking attention and should be left alone.
answer_b: Resident has a diagnosis that the CNA identified from the vital signs.
answer_c: No report is needed until the next scheduled charting time.
answer_d: Resident reports chest tightness 7/10; pulse 104; respirations 24; skin appears pale; nurse notified immediately.
rationale: Pain can affect vital signs and may signal urgent change. The best CNA report includes the pain rating, pulse, respirations, color observation, and immediate notification without diagnosis.

question_text: A resident has labored respirations and bluish lips. What is the safest sequence for the CNA?
answer_a: Notify the licensed nurse or emergency chain immediately, support safety within the care plan, then document objective facts according to policy.
answer_b: Complete all routine documentation before telling anyone.
answer_c: Wait to see whether the next shift notices the same signs.
answer_d: Chart that the resident had pneumonia because the breathing looked abnormal.
rationale: Urgent respiratory findings require immediate reporting before routine charting. Documentation follows and must remain factual and within CNA scope.

question_text: Which statement about U02 completion and certificates is compliance-safe while the course is Draft / Pending CDPH Approval?
answer_a: Passing the U02 quiz alone releases a certificate immediately.
answer_b: Certificate release remains blocked until required unit evidence, assessment gates, identity/no-PHI attestations, approval metadata, and administrative approval-status gates are satisfied.
answer_c: Clinical or simulation hours are awarded after the U02 scenario.
answer_d: Learners should upload real facility vital-sign sheets as proof of participation.
rationale: U02 is online CE only. Certificates must remain unavailable until all Moodle evidence and approval-status requirements are satisfied; real forms, PHI, clinical hours, and simulation claims are excluded.
